Claim trace: Title: How (and Whether) to Teach Undergraduates About the Replication Crisis in Psychological Science
Claim trace: Abstract: Pre- and post-lecture surveys suggest that the lecture serves as an excellent pedagogical tool. Following the lecture, students trusted psychological studies slightly less but saw greater similarities between psychology and natural science fields.
Claim trace: Hypothesis: (I did not spot an explicit hypothesis that maps directly to the statistical result. Thus, we set out to fill this void by developing an effective, up-to-date, and balanced treatment of the replication crisis with a full lecture script and prepackaged 1-hr PowerPoint presentation. We then tested its efficacy at both a large research university with very large class sizes and a mid-sized liberal arts college with very small class sizes.

Claim trace: Abstract: We found that answering questions about texts (retrieval practice) that were read with and without multitasking, both led to better recall 7 days later than texts read with multitasking followed by rereading..
Claim trace: Hypothesis: Hypothesis 2 was our main one of interest, which tested whether retrieval practice could counteract some of the negative long-term effects of multitasking while being exposed to information. It proposed that delayed recall (7 days after exposure and reexpo- sure to information) would be higher in the two conditions that included retrieval practice (mul- titask + retrieval and no multitask + retrieval) compared to the condition with rereading (mul- titask + reread), and also that participants would  perform better in the read-only (no multitask + retrieval) than the read while multitasking con- ditions (multitask + retrieval) when both were followed by retrieval practice.
Key claim (AI): Also as predicted, we found a positive retrieval practice effect 1 week later (Hypothesis 2; Figure 2).
Final claim: We found that answering questions about texts (retrieval practice) that were read with and without multitasking, both led to better recall 7 days later than texts read with multitasking followed by rereading..

Final claim: Students in asynchronous sections had a higher risk of withdrawing, as did students with lower self-efficacy and those reporting family obligations.

Claim trace: Abstract: Exam performance for those students who opted in was improved beyond what was predicted by their baseline trajectory.

Claim trace: Title: “Not Just a Number”: Promoting Belonging Through an In-Class Activity in a Large Course.

Claim trace: Title: The Testing Effect in the Lecture Hall: Does it Transfer to Content Studied but Not Practiced?
Claim trace: Abstract: We found a testing effect for practiced content, whereas no transfer effect occurred for untested content from the same lecture sessions.
Claim trace: Hypothesis: However, this testing effect should be stronger for questions that are directly tested in the practice tests compared to questions that refer to content from the same lecture sessions, but which are not also part of the practice tests or the restudy material (Hypothesis 2).
Key claim (AI): More specifically, a testing effect occurred only for content explicitly tested and no transfer effect to untested content from the same lecture occurred.
Final claim: We found a testing effect for practiced content, whereas no transfer effect occurred for untested content from the same lecture sessions.

Claim trace: Abstract: In Experiment 1, more positive evaluations were provided for SETs with higher ratings

Claim trace: Title: Understanding of Psychological Literacy: Comparative Insights From Undergraduates in China and the United Kingdom
Claim trace: Abstract: Students gave the highest ratings to the importance of psychological core attributes in their future careers and understanding the application of theory, but the lowest to the development of attributes and understanding the role of theory in research.
Claim trace: Hypothesis: (I did not spot an explicit hypothesis that maps directly to the statistical result. Key claim (AI): This study provides the first descriptive and cross-cultural investigation of perceptions of psychological literacy among Chinese undergraduate psychology majors, revealing that while students rate the importance of psychological literacy highly, they report significantly lower development of these attributes compared to their UK counterparts.
Final claim: Students gave the highest ratings to the importance of psychological core attributes in their future careers and understanding the application of theory, but the lowest to the development of attributes and understanding the role of theory in research.

Claim trace: Title: Modernizing the Flipped Classroom: Replacing Lecture Time With Asynchronous Spaced Retrieval
Claim trace: Abstract: On the final test of knowledge transfer, participants performed equally well on lectured and nonlectured questions.
Claim trace: Hypothesis: Further, we explored whether participants, after learning via retrieval practice, would demonstrate knowledge transfer on a final memory test. If successful, course content could be delivered to students via retrieval practice asynchronously and without an accompanying lecture, simultaneously reducing time burdens on both the student and instructor.
Key claim (AI): “Importantly, spaced retrieval alone appeared to successfully convey course content without a preceding lecture. Final claim: On the final test of knowledge transfer, participants performed equally well on lectured and nonlectured questions.

Claim trace: Abstract: Replicating previous research, recognition significantly benefited early eminent White male psychologists compared to women and psychologists of color.

Final claim: Replicating previous research, recognition significantly benefited early eminent White male psychologists compared to women and psychologists of color.

Claim trace: Title: Students Are Open to Dealing With a Changing Discipline
Claim trace: Abstract: The experimental groups working on material covering phases of Kuhn's model or the replication crisis showed no decline in expectancy, value, interest, or intention.
Claim trace: Hypothesis: When experimental material suggests that a relevant part to learn about psychology is learning about how psychology is changing, students might adopt to this and show higher levels of value, expectancy, interest and intention to deal with change of the discipline.
Key claim (AI): “We experimentally varied the perspectives on the (need for) change that students were confronted with. Final claim: The experimental groups working on material covering phases of Kuhn's model or the replication crisis showed no decline in expectancy, value, interest, or intention.
